authors: Michael L. Kasavana & Donald I. Smith
title: Menu Engineering: A Practical Guide to Menu Analysis
finding: Introduced the menu-engineering matrix: each item is plotted by sales volume (popularity) vs contribution margin, yielding four classes — Stars (high/high), Plowhorses (high popularity/low margin), Puzzles (low popularity/high margin), Dogs (low/low) — each with a prescribed action (promote, reprice, reposition, remove).

authors: David V. Pavesic
title: Cost/margin analysis: a third approach to menu pricing and design
publication: International Journal of Hospitality Management, 2(3); DOI 10.1016/0278-4319(83)90033-6
finding: Refined Kasavana & Smith by combining item food-cost percentage with the WEIGHTED contribution margin (margin weighted by units sold), so popularity enters the margin itself. Items classify as Primes, Standards, Sleepers and Problems; the goal is a sales mix that lowers overall food-cost % while maximizing total gross profit.

authors: Sybil S. Yang, Sheryl E. Kimes & Mauro M. Sessarego
title: $ or Dollars: Effects of Menu-price Formats on Restaurant Checks
publication: Cornell Hospitality Report, Vol. 9 No. 8, Cornell Center for Hospitality Research
finding: Field experiment at the Culinary Institute of America's St. Andrew's Cafe comparing three price formats: '$20.00', '20.' (numeral only), and 'twenty dollars' written out. Guests given the numeral-only menu spent significantly more than either the dollar-sign or written-out groups; a monetary cue ($ or the word) appears to trigger the 'pain of paying'.
citable_stat: Numeral-only prices (no $ symbol, no decimals): parties spent on average $5.55 more, about 8% more per check

authors: Joel Huber, John W. Payne & Christopher Puto
title: Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives: Violations of Regularity and the Similarity Hypothesis
finding: The original decoy / asymmetric-dominance paper: adding a third option that is clearly worse than one target (but not worse than the competitor) INCREASES the choice share of the target it is dominated by, violating the regularity axiom of rational choice. Demonstrated across six product categories (beer, cars, restaurants, film, lotteries, TV sets).

authors: Brian Wansink, James Painter & Koert van Ittersum
title: Descriptive Menu Labels' Effect on Sales
publication: Cornell Hotel and Restaurant Administration Quarterly, 42(6); DOI 10.1177/0010880401426008
finding: Six-week field experiment (university cafeteria, 140 diners): adding descriptive labels (geographic 'Traditional Cajun Red Beans', nostalgic 'Grandma's Zucchini Cookies', sensory 'Satin Chocolate Pudding') raised sales of the labeled items and improved attitudes toward food and restaurant plus repatronage intent. Caveat: it did NOT increase willingness to pay more for the item.
citable_stat: Descriptive menu labels increased sales of the labeled items by 27%

authors: Attributed to 'Omnès' (attribution disputed: many trade sources say André Omnès; L'Hôtellerie-Restauration credits consultant Jean Toulemonde and derives 'omnes' from Latin 'all')
title: Les principes d'Omnès (menu price-structure rules)
publication: French hospitality trade doctrine; no locatable primary academic publication. Best secondary source: L'Hôtellerie-Restauration fiche pratique
finding: Four price-structure rules for a menu category: (1) range span — most expensive item no more than 2.5-3x the cheapest; (2) dispersion — split the range into three price bands; the middle band should hold at least as many items as the low and high bands combined; (3) supply/demand — average price actually paid divided by average price offered should sit around 0.9-1.0; (4) promotion — highlighted/promoted items should sit near the category's average price.
citable_stat: Highest/lowest price ratio within a menu category should not exceed 2.5-3

authors: Sybil S. Yang
title: Eye movements on restaurant menus: A revisitation on gaze motion and consumer scanpaths
finding: Infrared retinal eye-tracking on mock two-page menus: diners read menus sequentially like a book (left page top-to-bottom, then right), slowly and for information, rather than jumping to a 'sweet spot'. Found no support for the classic 'golden triangle' / upper-right sweet-spot doctrine; gaze did not concentrate above the midline of the right page.
citable_stat: No evidence of a menu 'sweet spot'; diners scanned sequentially like reading a book

authors: David B. Strohmetz, Bruce Rind, Reed Fisher & Michael Lynn
title: Sweetening the Till: The Use of Candy to Increase Restaurant Tipping
finding: Two field experiments: giving candy with the check raised tip percentages via reciprocity, and the effect scaled with amount and especially with HOW it was offered — the biggest lift came when the server gave one candy per guest, turned away, then spontaneously came back and offered a second ('personalized' gift).
citable_stat: Relative tip increases: +3.3% (one candy), +14.1% (two candies), largest (~+21-23%) in the '1+1 spontaneous extra' condition (secondary sources report ~19.0% control vs ~23.0% tip)

authors: Bruce Rind & Prashant Bordia
title: Effect of Server's "Thank You" and Personalization on Restaurant Tipping
finding: Field experiment in an upscale US restaurant: the server wrote nothing, 'Thank you', or 'Thank you' + her first name on the back of the check. 'Thank you' raised tip percentages; adding her name gave no extra lift. NOTE: this is the 1995 paper — the same authors' 1996 paper is the smiley-face-on-check study.
citable_stat: Writing 'Thank you' on the check raised tips ~11% in relative terms (about 2 percentage points, ~16.3% to ~18.1% per secondary sources)

authors: April H. Crusco & Christopher G. Wetzel
title: The Midas Touch: The Effects of Interpersonal Touch on Restaurant Tipping
publication: Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 10(4), 512-517; DOI 10.1177/0146167284104003
finding: Waitresses briefly touched customers on the hand or shoulder while returning change, or did not touch. Both touch conditions produced significantly higher tip percentages than no touch, with no difference by customer gender and no change in ratings of waitress or restaurant — the effect operated without awareness.
citable_stat: Touch raised tips from ~12% (no touch) to ~14% (shoulder) and ~17% (hand touch) — rounded figures from secondary sources

authors: Kimberly Garrity & Douglas Degelman
title: Effect of Server Introduction on Restaurant Tipping
finding: Field experiment with 42 two-person Sunday-brunch parties: when the server introduced herself by first name, tip rate was dramatically higher than without introduction (p < .001). Small single-server study, so treat the size of the gap as indicative rather than universal.
citable_stat: Tips of 23.4% with a name introduction vs 15.0% without

authors: Rick B. van Baaren, Rob W. Holland, Bregje Steenaert & Ad van Knippenberg
title: Mimicry for money: Behavioral consequences of imitation
finding: Two Dutch restaurant experiments: a waitress who literally repeated customers' orders back (verbal mimicry) received larger and more frequent tips than when she paraphrased or just acknowledged. Replicated with a hypothesis-blind waitress in Experiment 2 (78% vs 52% of groups tipped; average tip 2.73 vs 1.36 guilders).
citable_stat: Repeating the order back increased the average tip by more than 68% (Exp. 1: 2.97 vs 1.76 guilders; verified in primary PDF)

authors: Michael Lynn & Michael McCall
title: Gratitude and gratuity: a meta-analysis of research on the service-tipping relationship
finding: Meta-analysis of 13 studies (2,547 dining parties, 20 restaurants): the correlation between service evaluations and tip size is positive and statistically significant but MUCH weaker than commonly assumed — tips are a poor incentive/measure of service quality; situational and social factors dominate.
citable_stat: Positive but weak service-tip relationship across 2,547 dining parties in 20 restaurants

authors: Michael Lynn & Michael McCall
title: Techniques for Increasing Servers' Tips: How Generalizable Are They?
finding: Internet survey of 1,066 US restaurant servers: those who more frequently used experimentally-tested tip-enhancing behaviors (introducing by name, squatting at the table, writing on checks, small gifts, etc.) reported larger tips relative to coworkers, across many server and restaurant types — supporting the generalizability of the field-experiment toolkit Lynn compiled in 'Mega Tips'.
citable_stat: Lynn's companion 'Mega Tips' manual estimates using several techniques together can raise a server's tips by 10-30%

authors: Ronald E. Milliman
title: The Influence of Background Music on the Behavior of Restaurant Patrons
finding: Field experiment in a mid-Atlantic restaurant alternating slow- vs fast-tempo background music on weekend nights: slow tempo lengthened the meal and markedly raised bar/beverage revenue, while food revenue was essentially unchanged. Estimated gross margin was higher on slow-music nights.
citable_stat: Slow music: 56 min average dinner vs 45 min with fast music; bar/drink spend up ~40% (~3 more drinks per table); food sales not significantly different

authors: Adrian C. North, David J. Hargreaves & Jennifer McKendrick
title: The influence of in-store music on wine selections (full report; brief report 'In-store music affects product choice', Nature 390:132, 1997)
publication: Journal of Applied Psychology, 84(2), 271-276; Nature 390, 132 (1997), DOI 10.1038/36484
finding: Supermarket field study: French and German wines displayed side by side while French accordion or German Bierkeller music alternated daily for two weeks. Music nationality steered purchases toward the matching country's wine, and shoppers were largely unaware of (or denied) the influence.
citable_stat: French-music days: 40 French vs 8 German bottles sold (5:1); German-music days: 22 German vs 12 French (2:1); only 6 of 44 questioned shoppers acknowledged the music's influence

authors: Betina Piqueras-Fiszman, Vanessa Harrar, Jorge Alcaide & Charles Spence
title: Does the weight of the dish influence our perception of food?
finding: Participants tasted identical yogurt from visually identical bowls differing only in weight (held in hand): yogurt from the heaviest bowl was rated denser, expected to be more expensive, and was liked more; perceived flavour intensity did not change. The tactile weight of what the diner holds (bowls, and by extension menus, bottles, cutlery in the authors' related studies) transfers to perceived quality and price.
citable_stat: Same yogurt from a heavier bowl was rated denser, pricier-seeming and better liked than from a lighter bowl

authors: Vincent P. Magnini & Seontaik Kim
title: The influences of restaurant menu font style, background color, and physical weight on consumers' perceptions
finding: 2x2x2 experiment (italic vs plain font, gold vs white background, heavy vs light menu): a physically HEAVIER menu increased perceptions that the restaurant is upscale and will deliver better service; italic font had a similar upscale effect; background color (gold vs white) showed no significant effect. This is the study to cite for 'menu weight signals quality' (the Piqueras-Fiszman/Spence line covers plateware, bottles and cutlery).
citable_stat: Heavier menus significantly raised perceived upscaleness and anticipated service quality; gold vs white background made no difference

authors: Manoj Thomas & Vicki Morwitz
title: Penny Wise and Pound Foolish: The Left-Digit Effect in Price Cognition
finding: Five experiments showing WHY charm prices work: 9-ending prices are perceived as smaller than a price one cent higher ONLY when the left-most digit changes ($2.99 vs $3.00 — yes; $3.59 vs $3.60 — no), because magnitude encoding starts with the left digit. The effect strengthens when compared prices are numerically close.
citable_stat: The 1-cent drop matters only when it changes the left digit ($3.00 -> $2.99), not otherwise ($3.60 -> $3.59)

authors: Monica Wadhwa & Kuangjie Zhang
title: This Number Just Feels Right: The Impact of Roundedness of Price Numbers on Product Evaluations
finding: Five studies: rounded prices (200.00) are processed fluently and 'feel right' when the purchase is feeling-driven (hedonic, celebratory — e.g. champagne for a holiday), increasing purchase likelihood; precise, non-rounded prices (198.76) fit cognition-driven, utilitarian evaluations better. Rationale for round prices on fine-dining/experience menus and charm prices in value contexts.
citable_stat: Rounded prices boost purchase intent for feeling-based (hedonic) purchases; non-rounded prices for cognition-based ones — a fluency ('feels right') mediation

authors: Sheena S. Iyengar & Mark R. Lepper
title: When Choice is Demotivating: Can One Desire Too Much of a Good Thing?
publication: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 79(6), 995-1006
finding: The 'jam study' and two follow-ups: an extensive display (24 jams) attracted more browsers than a limited one (6 jams), but conversion collapsed with more options. IMPORTANT NUANCE: subsequent meta-analysis (Scheibehenne et al. 2010) found the average choice-overload effect across 50 experiments is near zero — the effect is real only under moderating conditions (unfamiliar options, no dominant option, time pressure, no clear preferences).
citable_stat: ~30% of tasters bought jam at the 6-option booth vs ~3% at the 24-option booth (verified in the paper: 31 vs 4 buyers)

authors: Benjamin Scheibehenne, Rainer Greifeneder & Peter M. Todd
title: Can There Ever Be Too Many Options? A Meta-Analytic Review of Choice Overload
finding: Meta-analysis of 63 conditions from 50 experiments (N = 5,036): the mean effect size of 'choice overload' is virtually zero, with large between-study variance. Choice overload is not a law — it appears only under specific conditions (option complexity, lack of expertise/preferences, no dominant option). The honest counterweight to the jam study.
citable_stat: Mean choice-overload effect size across 50 experiments (N=5,036): virtually zero

authors: Eran Dayan & Maya Bar-Hillel
title: Nudge to Nobesity II: Menu Positions Influence Food Orders
finding: One lab and one real-restaurant study manipulating where items sit WITHIN their menu category list: items at the beginning or end of their category were substantially more chosen than the same items placed mid-list. Empirical support for placing high-margin items at category edges (and a within-category effect — they did not test across categories).
citable_stat: Items at the top or bottom of their category list were up to twice as popular as the same items in the middle (~55% vs 45% of selections at category level)

authors: Irwin P. Levin & Gary J. Gaeth
title: How Consumers Are Affected by the Framing of Attribute Information Before and After Consuming the Product
finding: Classic attribute-framing study: ground beef labeled '75% lean' was rated better tasting and less greasy than identical beef labeled '25% fat'. The framing effect shrank (but persisted) after actually tasting the product. Foundation for positive-attribute framing in menu copy.
citable_stat: '75% lean' beat '25% fat' on all evaluation dimensions for the identical product; effect diluted but not erased by tasting

authors: Brian Wansink, Koert van Ittersum & James E. Painter
title: How descriptive food names bias sensory perceptions in restaurants
finding: Companion to the 2001 sales study: evocative names ('Succulent Italian Seafood Filet' vs 'Seafood Filet') made the identical dish taste better, look more appealing and even seem more caloric, and doubled positive post-meal comments. Descriptive labels shift the sensory experience itself, not just the click/order decision.
citable_stat: Descriptive names raised taste ratings (7.31 vs 6.83) and appeal (6.66 vs 5.87) for identical dishes; positive comments doubled

authors: European Parliament and Council
title: Reglamento (UE) n.º 1169/2011 sobre la información alimentaria facilitada al consumidor — Anexo II
publication: Diario Oficial de la Unión Europea L 304, 22.11.2011 (Anexo II, p. L304/43)
finding: Annex II lists the 14 substances causing allergies/intolerances whose declaration is mandatory (including in non-prepacked food served by restaurants, per Art. 44; in Spain implemented by RD 126/2015): 1 cereales con gluten (trigo, centeno, cebada, avena, espelta, kamut), 2 crustáceos, 3 huevos, 4 pescado, 5 cacahuetes, 6 soja, 7 leche (incl. lactosa), 8 frutos de cáscara (almendras, avellanas, nueces, anacardos, pacanas, nueces de Brasil, pistachos, macadamias), 9 apio, 10 mostaza, 11 granos de sésamo, 12 dióxido de azufre y sulfitos (>10 mg/kg o mg/l SO2), 13 altramuces, 14 moluscos.
citable_stat: 14 mandatory-declaration allergens (Anexo II), verified against the Official Journal text

authors: Parlament de Catalunya
title: Ley 20/1985, de 25 de julio, de prevención y asistencia en materia de sustancias que pueden generar dependencia — artículo 15.2
publication: DOGC / BOE (texto consolidado BOE-A-1985-18553); apartado 15.2 añadido por la Ley 18/2009, de 22 de octubre, de salud pública de Cataluña (d.f. 1.1)
finding: Article 15.2 (consolidated text) prohibits in Catalonia the promotion of alcoholic drinks in bars/restaurants/venues via offers announced as 'barra libre', '2x1', '3x1' or similar that incite abusive or unlimited consumption, and bans advertising such promotions by any means. Happy-hour-style alcohol deals fall under this prohibition; the wording was introduced in 2009, not in the original 1985 text.
citable_stat: Art. 15.2: 'Se prohíbe la promoción de bebidas alcohólicas... mediante ofertas que se anuncian con nombres como "barra libre", "2x1", "3x1", o similares, que inciten al consumo abusivo o ilimitado' (verified in BOE consolidated text)

authors: Cortes Generales (España)
title: Ley 7/2022, de 8 de abril, de residuos y suelos contaminados para una economía circular — artículo 18.3
publication: BOE-A-2022-5809, en vigor desde el 10 de abril de 2022
finding: Article 18.3 (waste-prevention measures) obliges hospitality and restaurant establishments to ALWAYS offer customers the possibility of consuming non-bottled (tap) water free of charge, complementary to the establishment's own offer. Selling bottled water remains legal, but the free option must exist and no direct or indirect charge (glass, ice, service) may be applied.
citable_stat: Art. 18.3: '...en los establecimientos del sector de la hostelería y restauración se tendrá que ofrecer siempre a los consumidores... la posibilidad de consumo de agua no envasada de manera gratuita y complementaria a la oferta del mismo establecimiento'
